excerpt: There are many instances in Islamic history in which the elders restrained themselves for fear of retribution and forgave the wrongdoer in the way of Allah.
It has been narrated that when the Imam passed away to the mercy of Allah, the mare came to his house looking for him. When he was not there, she went to his shrine and placed her head on his grave.
source_work: Biographical accounts of Imam Zayn al-ʿAbidin

excerpt: The Imam said, 'I pay the retribution for the slap I gave you by dedicating this house to you in the way of Allah.'
Explaining the greatness of Allah, He says: 'If it were not for you, I would not have created this heaven.'
Maulana al-Dāʿī al-ʿAbdān describes that in this period the Lord served his slave, as all prophets from Adam suffered and fought on the orders of the Imams.
source_location_hint: Doctrinal writings on the hierarchy of revelation

excerpt: It is in the world of man after every thousand years his world is reversed. The other of its previous sphere becomes the fate for the current one.
There came two persons to David's house, one of whom had a cow. The first person claimed the second that the cow was his, but the other man refused and presented evidence and witnesses.
source_location_hint: Story of Prophet Dāwūd and the judgments of the hereafter

excerpt: An old man came holding a young man by the arm, complaining that the young man had entered his garden and spoiled his cultivation.
O Dāwūd, these were my three decisions of the hereafter. I have saved all such decisions for the day of reckoning.
source_location_hint: Story of Prophet Dāwūd's three judgments

excerpt: The Imams killed people, flogging and stoning people by the command of Allah, and although all these actions were under the commands of the Shariah, they did not involve the consent of the victim. Therefore, retribution was obligatory on these Imams.
When Amīr al-Muʾminīn ʿAlī would flog someone, for each blow his garment would be altered. The reward for such punishment falls on them and elevates their status.
source_work: Biographical and spiritual accounts of Imam ʿAlī
source_location_hint: Teachings on the spiritual dimensions of punishment

excerpt: A preacher can predict a person's future because they are aware of that person's power and coercion. When a preacher predicts that someone will be killed, he sees the moment of death and the killer's bad intentions.
source_location_hint: Doctrine of divine knowledge and free will

excerpt: Imam Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq witnessed a swine being stoned by children. The woman who poured water on him was his sister in a previous existence; his family had falsely accused her of adultery, but only her brother believed in her innocence.
source_work: Biographical accounts of Imam Jaʿfar al-Ṣādiq
source_location_hint: Stories illustrating the doctrine of retribution

excerpt: Just as the order of the Qur'an was altered, so was the order of Maulana ʿAlī altered. Had he not remained silent, both the Qur'an and Islam would have been destroyed.
source_location_hint: Doctrine of ʿAlī and the Qur'an

excerpt: When Ibn Muljam struck the sword on Amīr al-Muʾminīn's head on the nineteenth of Ramaḍān, these words escaped his lips: 'I have succeeded, by the Lord of the Kaʿbah!'
source_location_hint: Narrative of ʿAlī's death and its meaning

excerpt: Imam al-Ḥusayn felt the burden he was destined to discharge—the settlement of all debts that had accrued since the time of Adam, and the recompense owed to his parents.
source_work: Accounts of Imam al-Ḥusayn's life and mission

excerpt: The Prophet declared: 'O al-Ḥusayn, I bestow upon you a rank that shall not be attained save through martyrdom.' Al-Ḥusayn responded: 'O Allah, make me among the grateful.'
We mourn because these people slew him believing him to be al-Ḥusayn—the one whom the Prophet loved dearly, a piece of Fāṭimah's liver.
source_work: Devotional and exegetical commentary on Karbalā
